Before scaling our theoretical framework, let us unify the structural nomenclature of the Encoder-Decoder Matrix Processing Pipeline.

In machine learning theory, an unsupervised network that maps features through an intermediate hidden space is governed by a strict two-part operational definition:

  1. The Encoder Network (\(E_\phi\)): A mapping function (linear projection or deep neural network) that projects coordinates from the native high-dimensional data space \(\mathbb{X} \in \mathbb{R}^D\) down into the hidden latent manifold space \(\mathbb{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^K\).
  2. The Decoder Network (\(D_\theta\)): A mapping function that projects latent coordinates from \(\mathbb{Z}\) back into the native data space \(\mathbb{R}^D\).

Because the output of a decoder function generates physical coordinates within the original data domain, every decoder network operates fundamentally as a data sampler.

  [ Data Space X ] ──────────> ( Encoder Network E_ϕ ) ──────────> [ Latent Space Z ]
                                                                          │
  [ Reconstructed Space X̂ ] <─── ( Decoder Network D_θ ) <────────────────┘

This structural separation defines the primary deployment archetypes of modern architectures:

  • Encoder-Decoder Architectures (e.g., Classical BERT): Houses both operational paths. Given a data matrix, it can compute a hidden embedding vector; given a hidden embedding vector, it can reconstruct the data matrix back.
  • Decoder-Only Architectures (e.g., Generative Autoregressive LLMs): Discards the explicit encoder transformation track (\(X \to Z\)). The model operates entirely in the continuous latent token space, projecting hidden coordinates forward to sample physical words or pixels directly.

docs.title: The Variational Autoencoder (VAE) Regularization Identity
docs.text:
When an unconstrained autoencoder runs its training pass, the continuous latent variable \(Z\) is free to take any arbitrary coordinate value because its distribution is unconstrained, depending entirely on the frozen encoder parameters.

To control this latent manifold and force it to conform to a predictable distribution envelope, we can introduce Activity Regularization directly to the hidden representation layer.

This design pattern defines the foundation of the Variational Autoencoder (VAE). A VAE forces the network to minimize its reconstruction error while simultaneously minimizing a strict Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence penalty that constrains the empirical distribution of \(Z\) to match a pre-selected target prior density function (such as a standard normal Gaussian \(\mathcal{N}(\vec{0}, \mathbf{I})\)):

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}(\phi, \,\, \theta) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\vec{z} \mid \vec{x})}\left[ \ln P_\theta(\vec{x} \mid \vec{z}) \right] - D_{\text{KL}}\Big( q_\phi(\vec{z} \mid \vec{x}) \parallel P(\vec{z}) \Big)\]

docs.title: 47.2 The Informational Complexity Asymmetry: Conditionals vs. Marginals
docs.text:
We now transition to a fundamentally different paradigm of unsupervised estimation designed to solve the deepest bottleneck in generative modeling: the intractability of high-dimensional density normalization.

Let us evaluate the raw computational complexity separating our two primary distributional tracking targets: the Conditional Label Density \(P(Y \mid X)\) and the Global Data Marginal Density \(P(X)\).

The professor states a foundational rule of statistical geometry: discriminative conditional models are inherently easier to train than generative marginal models. To understand why this asymmetry exists, we must analyze how each model structures its spatial partitions.

[Image demonstrating spatial classification hyperplane partitioning vs continuous probability density landscape modeling]

docs.title: The Discriminative Axis (\(P(Y \mid X)\))
docs.text:
When you train a binary classifier, your output target space is a compressed discrete density governed by a single scalar probability parameter. Geometrically, the model's only job is to calculate an axis hyperplane that partitions the high-dimensional manifold into non-overlapping regions.

As long as an observation coordinate falls on the correct side of that boundary wall, the model does not care where the point sits within that broad zone. The framework can safely confuse the exact coordinates of two distinct data points, because as long as they share the same class category, the model's empirical risk is minimized perfectly.

docs.title: The Generative Axis (\(P(X)\))
docs.text:
When you train a marginal density estimator, this spatial confusion is completely forbidden. The model cannot simply declare that a coordinate resides within a generalized region; it must compute the exact mathematical density profile at every continuous coordinate point across the entire manifold.

The engine must allocate precise probability mass across thousands of feature channels, ensuring that high-density clusters spike sharply while neighboring empty spaces drop to zero. Generative modeling requires mapping the absolute structural coordinates of the entire universe, scaling up the parameter complexity significantly relative to binary classification.

docs.title: 47.3 The Normalization Catastrophe of Energy-Based Models
docs.text:
To formalize why maximum likelihood estimation becomes computationally intractable under this generative axis, we look at the mathematical form of Energy-Based Models (EBMs) and Gibbs Distributions.

Suppose we assume our parametric data density model \(P_\theta(\vec{x})\) conforms to an exponential unnormalized structural layout:

\[P_\theta(\vec{x}) = \frac{P'_\theta(\vec{x})}{Z(\theta)}\]

docs.title: Precise Subspace Functional Typing:
docs.text:
• \(\vec{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1}\): The observed high-dimensional feature vector.
  • \(P'_\theta(\vec{x}): \mathbb{R}^D \to \mathbb{R}^+\): An unnormalized parametric function mapping our data space directly to positive real numbers (often parameterized as an exponential energy function \(e^{-E_\theta(\vec{x})}\)).
  • \(Z(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^+\): The global Normalization Constant, formally classified as the Partition Function.

To ensure that \(P_\theta(\vec{x})\) satisfies the foundational axioms of probability theory and integrates to exactly unity (\(\int P_\theta(\vec{x}) d\vec{x} = 1.0\)), the partition function \(Z(\theta)\) must calculate the total accumulated volume of our unnormalized function across the entire continuous data domain:

\[Z(\theta) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^D} P'_\theta(\vec{x}) \, d\vec{x}\]

docs.title: The Intractability Bottleneck
docs.text:
When we attempt to optimize this model via standard Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), we compute the log-likelihood function across a dataset of \(n\) samples, unrolling the partition function explicitly:

\[\ln L( heta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln P_ heta(\vec{x}_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln P'_ heta(\vec{x}_i) - n \ln Z( heta)\]

Taking the gradient of this log-likelihood requires differentiating the partition function:

\[ abla_ heta \ln Z( heta) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^D} rac{ abla_ heta P'_ heta(\vec{x})}{Z( heta)} \, d\vec{x} = \int_{\mathbb{R}^D} abla_ heta \ln P'_ heta(\vec{x}) \cdot P_ heta(\vec{x}) \, d\vec{x} = \mathbb{E}_{\vec{x} \sim P_ heta(\vec{x})} \left[ abla_ heta \ln P'_ heta(\vec{x}) ight]\]

Look at the severe computational bottleneck of this gradient step: evaluating this mathematical expectation requires computing an integral across the entire high-dimensional continuous space (\(\mathbb{R}^D\)). When the feature dimension \(D\) spans thousands of channels (such as raw pixel grids or vocabulary matrices), this integration becomes completely intractable. The partition function \(Z( heta)\) cannot be solved analytically, blocking standard maximum likelihood estimation completely.

docs.title: 47.4 Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE): The Classification Framework
docs.text:
To resolve this normalization catastrophe, Gutmann and Hyvärinen introduced Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE).

The core thesis of NCE is a brilliant conceptual inversion: Can we convert the complex problem of generative marginal density estimation into a simple binary classification problem, allowing us to learn the true data density \(P_X\) using standard discriminative loss functions?

docs.title: The Contrastive Philosophy
docs.text:
The philosophical premise of NCE is straightforward: If an architecture can flawlessly differentiate a structural signal (music) from a completely unstructured background distribution (noise), it must inherently understand the structural layout of that signal. By training a model to discriminate between real data samples and artificial noise samples, the network implicitly learns the absolute density profile of the true data manifold.

We formalize the NCE framework step-by-step from absolute first principles.

docs.title: Step 1: Defining the Dual Density Spaces
docs.text:
We define our primary target space as the true, unknown data distribution \(P_X(\vec{x})\). We collect a clean, empirical dataset \(D\) containing exactly \(N\) samples harvested from this distribution:

\[\mathcal{D} = \{\vec{x}_1, \,\, \vec{x}_2, \,\, \dots, \,\, \vec{x}_N\} \quad \sim \quad P_X(\vec{x})\]

Next, we explicitly introduce a secondary, completely artificial reference distribution known as the Noise Density Function (\(P_N(\vec{x})\)). This noise density can be any arbitrary, known distribution (such as a simple Gaussian or a uniform box) that we can easily sample from, under the sole condition that it is not identical to our true data distribution:

\[P_N(\vec{x}) \neq P_X(\vec{x})\]

We deploy this reference model to sample exactly \(M\) independent artificial noise data points, compiling a secondary dataset denoted as \(D'\):

\[\mathcal{D}' = \{\vec{y}_1, \,\, \vec{y}_2, \,\, \dots, \,\, \vec{y}_M\} \quad \sim \quad P_N(\vec{x})\]

docs.title: Step 2: Constructing the Extended Union Manifold (\(D_U\))
docs.text:
We merge these two independent datasets together side-by-side, constructing an expanded Union Dataset Matrix (\(D_U\)) containing a total headcount of \(N + M\) coordinate points:

\[\mathcal{D}_U = \mathcal{D} \cup \mathcal{D}' = \{\vec{u}_1, \,\, \vec{u}_2, \,\, \dots, \,\, \vec{u}_N, \,\, \vec{u}_{N+1}, \,\, \dots, \,\, \vec{u}_{N+M}\}\]

docs.title: Step 3: Enforcing the Binary Labeling Strategy
docs.text:
We turn this unsupervised data pool into a supervised pipeline by assigning an explicit, discrete Binary Class Label (\(C_j\)) to every individual coordinate slot inside our union dataset \(D_U\):

\[C_j = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vec{u}_j \in \mathcal{D} \quad \text{(Real Data Sample)} \\ 0 & \text{if } \vec{u}_j \in \mathcal{D}' \quad \text{(Artificial Noise Sample)} \end{cases}\]

docs.title: 47.5 Rigorous Derivation of the NCE Posterior Equations
docs.text: By framing our data layout using this binary structure, we can map out our system's underlying probability mechanics with absolute mathematical precision.

docs.title: 1. The Class-Conditional Densities
docs.text:
By definition, if a coordinate point is drawn from Class 1, its spatial distribution matches our parametric model density \(P_\theta(\vec{u})\). If it is drawn from Class 0, its distribution matches our noise density \(P_N(\vec{u})\):

\[P(\vec{u} \mid C = 1) = P_\theta(\vec{u})\] \[P(\vec{u} \mid C = 0) = P_N(\vec{u})\]

docs.title: 2. The Statistical Prior Probabilities
docs.text:
The prior probability of randomly plucking a sample that belongs to Class 1 or Class 0 from our union pool \(D_U\) depends entirely on the relative sample sizes of our two datasets:

\[P(C = 1) = \frac{N}{N + M}\] \[P(C = 0) = \frac{M}{N + M}\]

docs.title: 3. The Bayesian Posterior Derivation
docs.text:
We now invoke Bayes' Law to calculate the exact posterior probability that an arbitrary coordinate point \(\vec{u}\) pulled from our union dataset originates from the true data distribution (\(C=1\)):

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{P(\vec{u} \mid C = 1)P(C = 1)}{P(\vec{u} \mid C = 1)P(C = 1) + P(\vec{u} \mid C = 0)P(C = 0)}\]

We substitute our class-conditional definitions and statistical priors straight into this equation:

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{P_ heta(\vec{u}) \cdot \left( rac{N}{N + M} ight)}{P_ heta(\vec{u}) \cdot \left( rac{N}{N + M} ight) + P_N(\vec{u}) \cdot \left( rac{M}{N + M} ight)}\]

To clear the fraction, we multiply both the numerator and the denominator by the global sample denominator \((N + M)\):

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{N \cdot P_ heta(\vec{u})}{N \cdot P_ heta(\vec{u}) + M \cdot P_N(\vec{u})}\]

Finally, we isolate our model density by dividing both the numerator and the denominator by the scalar headcount variable \(N\):

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{ rac{N}{N} \cdot P_ heta(\vec{u})}{ rac{N}{N} \cdot P_ heta(\vec{u}) + rac{M}{N} \cdot P_N(\vec{u})} = rac{P_ heta(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u}) + \left( rac{M}{N} ight) P_N(\vec{u})}\]

To simplify our notation, we define a permanent scalar parameter tracking the exact ratio of noise samples to data samples, denoted as the Noise Index (\( u\)):

\[ u riangleq rac{M}{N}\]

Substituting this noise index \( u\) into our equation yields the definitive NCE Data Posterior Formula:

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{P_ heta(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u}) + u P_N(\vec{u})}\]

By following the exact same algebraic progression, we calculate the matching posterior probability that the coordinate point originates from our noise distribution (\(C=0\)):

\[P(C = 0 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{ u P_N(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u}) + u P_N(\vec{u})}\]

docs.title: 47.6 Unrolling the Binary Cross-Entropy Loss Architecture
docs.text:
We have derived the exact equations for our posterior probabilities. We now map the learning loop of NCE onto a standard Discriminative Binary Cross-Entropy Loss Function.

Our optimization goal is to update the model parameters \( heta\) to maximize the conditional log-likelihood of our binary class labels across all \(N+M\) samples stored within our union dataset \(D_U\):

\[\mathcal{L}( heta) = \sum_{j=1}^{N+M} \left[ C_j \ln P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}_j) + (1 - C_j) \ln P(C = 0 \mid \vec{u}_j) ight]\]

Because our class labels \(C_j\) are discrete binary indicators (\(1\) or \(0\)), we can unroll this global summation by splitting it back into its two independent source datasets: the \(N\) real data points (\(\vec{x}_i \in D\)) and the \(M\) artificial noise points (\(\vec{y}_i \in D'\)):

\[\mathcal{L}( heta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(C = 1 \mid \vec{x}_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \ln P(C = 0 \mid \vec{y}_i)\]

We substitute our derived Bayesian posterior formulas directly into this split loss ledger:

\[\mathcal{L}( heta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln \left( rac{P_ heta(\vec{x}_i)}{P_ heta(\vec{x}_i) + u P_N(\vec{x}_i)} ight) + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \ln \left( rac{ u P_N(\vec{y}_i)}{P_ heta(\vec{y}_i) + u P_N(\vec{y}_i)} ight)\]

docs.title: The Logistic Sigmoid Transformation
docs.text:
To highlight the connection between this framework and standard neural networks, we rewrite our posterior equation using a functional change of variables. Let us isolate our data posterior formula and divide both the numerator and the denominator by our model density \(P_ heta(\vec{u})\):

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{ rac{P_ heta(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u})}}{ rac{P_ heta(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u})} + u rac{P_N(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u})}} = rac{1}{1 + u \left( rac{P_N(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u})} ight)}\]

We move our variables into an exponential layer using standard logarithmic identities (\(A = e^{\ln A}\)):

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{1}{1 + \exp\left( \ln \left( u rac{P_N(\vec{u})}{P_ heta(\vec{u})} ight) ight)} = rac{1}{1 + \exp\left( \ln( u) + \ln P_N(\vec{u}) - \ln P_ heta(\vec{u}) ight)}\] \[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = rac{1}{1 + \exp\left( -\Big( \ln P_ heta(\vec{u}) - \ln P_N(\vec{u}) - \ln( u) \Big) ight)}\]

We define our core structural transformation variable as the Log-Density Difference Function (\(f_ heta(\vec{u})\)):

\[f_ heta(\vec{u}) riangleq \ln P_ heta(\vec{u}) - \ln P_N(\vec{u})\]

Substituting this function shape straight back into our equation yields:

\[P(C = 1 \mid \vec{u}) = \sigma\Big( f_ heta(\vec{u}) - \ln( u) \Big) \quad ext{where} \quad \sigma(z) = rac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\]

This is a beautiful result. It proves that our NCE data posterior equation matches the exact mathematical structure of a standard Logistic Sigmoid Activation Layer.

By training a neural network to model \(f_ heta(\vec{u})\) and passing the outputs through a sigmoid function, maximizing the binary cross-entropy loss over our data-noise pool allows the model to estimate the true underlying data density \(P_X\).

The network does not need to compute an intractable partition function integral. By framing unsupervised learning as a discriminative classification task, the model learns the data distribution implicitly, providing a powerful, scalable engine for training modern foundational artificial intelligence.

docs.title: 66.1 From Discriminative to Generative: A Shift in Goals
docs.text:
Throughout this course, the central object of study has been a fixed but unknown probability distribution \(p(\mathbf{x})\) defined over some space \(\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d\). We are never given \(p(\mathbf{x})\) directly. What we are given is a dataset

\[\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(n)}\}\]

where each \(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} p(\mathbf{x})\). Every method we have studied — maximum likelihood estimation, kernel density estimation, neural network classifiers, support vector machines — has been, at its mathematical core, an attempt to estimate \(p(\mathbf{x})\) or some functional of it from these \(n\) samples.

Recall the two broad regimes we identified:

Supervised (discriminative) learning estimates the conditional distribution \(p(y \mid \mathbf{x})\). We observe pairs \((\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\) and use them to construct an approximation \(p_\theta(y \mid \mathbf{x})\). A neural network trained with cross-entropy loss does this implicitly, without ever committing to a parametric family for the full joint \(p(\mathbf{x}, y)\).

Unsupervised learning estimates the marginal \(p(\mathbf{x})\) — the distribution of the data itself, with no labels.

Generative models live in the unsupervised regime, but they impose a strictly stronger requirement than mere distribution estimation:

Definition 66.1 (Goal of Generative Modeling). Given \(\mathcal{D} \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} p(\mathbf{x})\), a generative model must: 1. Estimate \(p(\mathbf{x})\), and 2. Provide an efficient algorithm to draw new samples \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p(\mathbf{x})\).

The second requirement is what distinguishes generative models from the discriminative and density-estimation methods we have already studied. A kernel density estimator, for instance, gives us an estimate \(\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})\) that we can evaluate at any point, but drawing a fresh sample from \(\hat{p}\) requires additional work. A generative model, by definition, is constructed so that sampling is a first-class operation.

docs.title: 66.2 Taxonomy: Explicit vs. Implicit Density Estimation
docs.text:
The same explicit/implicit dichotomy we introduced for discriminative models applies here.

Explicit generative models commit to a parametric family \(\{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) : \theta \in \Theta\}\) and fit \(\theta\) by some form of divergence minimization (most commonly maximum likelihood, which minimizes the KL divergence from the empirical distribution to the model). Once \(\theta\) is fit, sampling requires inverting or simulating the assumed family.

Example. A Gaussian mixture model (GMM) is an explicit generative model. We assume

\[p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)\]

with \(\theta = \{\pi_k, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k\}_{k=1}^K\). Maximum likelihood via the EM algorithm gives \(\hat\theta\). Sampling from \(p_{\hat\theta}\) is easy: draw a component \(k \sim \text{Categorical}(\hat\pi_1, \ldots, \hat\pi_K)\), then draw \(\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_k, \hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_k)\).

Implicit generative models never write down a tractable closed form for \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\). Instead, they define a procedure to generate samples, and the distribution that procedure induces on \(\mathcal{X}\) is \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\) — even if \(p_\theta\) has no closed-form expression. We will see that the most powerful modern architectures — GANs, VAEs, and autoregressive models — are all, in different ways, implicit or semi-implicit.

docs.title: 66.3 Conditional vs. Unconditional Generation
docs.text:
Just as supervised learning can be seen as conditional distribution estimation, generative models can operate conditionally or unconditionally.

Unconditional generation models and samples from the marginal \(p(\mathbf{x})\). The model receives no additional information specifying what kind of sample to produce. The website thispersonisnotexist.com is a canonical example: you send a random noise vector \(\mathbf{z}\) and receive a photorealistic human face sampled from \(p(\mathbf{x})\), the marginal distribution of human face images.

Conditional generation models and samples from a conditional distribution \(p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y})\), where \(\mathbf{y}\) is some conditioning variable (a text prompt, a class label, a partially observed image, etc.). Mathematically:

\[\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}_0)\]

where \(\mathbf{y}_0\) is the specific conditioning value provided at inference time.

Example: Text-to-image generation. Here \(\mathbf{y}\) is a text embedding (a vector representation of a caption such as "a cat sitting on a red sofa") and \(\mathbf{x}\) is a high-dimensional image vector. The model learns \(p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y})\) from millions of (caption, image) pairs.

Example: Large Language Models. An LLM is a conditional generative model over token sequences. Given a prompt \(\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \ldots, y_t)\), it models

\[p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y}) = p(x_{t+1}, x_{t+2}, \ldots \mid y_1, \ldots, y_t)\]

and generates by sequentially sampling one token at a time:

\[x_{t+1} \sim p(x_{t+1} \mid y_1, \ldots, y_t), \quad x_{t+2} \sim p(x_{t+2} \mid y_1, \ldots, y_t, x_{t+1}), \quad \ldots\]

This is the autoregressive sampling paradigm, and we will return to it in Section 66.8.

The mathematical tools for unconditional generation are foundational and apply, with minor modifications, to the conditional case as well. We therefore develop the unconditional theory first.

docs.title: 66.4 The Sampling Problem: A Precise Formulation
docs.text:
Before we can build generative models, we need to be precise about what it means to "sample from a distribution."

Recall from Chapter 1: a probability space is a triple \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P})\). A random variable \(X : \Omega \to \mathcal{X}\) is a measurable function from the sample space \(\Omega\) to the data space \(\mathcal{X}\). The distribution \(p(\mathbf{x})\) is the pushforward of \(\mathbb{P}\) through \(X\): for any measurable set \(A \subseteq \mathcal{X}\),

\[p(A) = \mathbb{P}(X^{-1}(A)) = \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega : X(\omega) \in A\})\]

Our data \(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{X}\) are elements of the range of \(X\). They came from conducting the underlying random experiment \(n\) times. To generate new data, we need to conduct that random experiment again — but we do not have access to \(\Omega\), to \(\mathbb{P}\), or to the experiment itself. All we have is \(\mathcal{D}\), the observed outputs \(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{X}\).

The sampling problem: Given \(\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^n\) drawn i.i.d. from an unknown \(p(\mathbf{x})\), produce new points \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{X}\) such that \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p(\mathbf{x})\) — that is, such that the law of \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\) is \(p\).

The phrase "such that the law of \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\) is \(p\)" is crucial. It is not sufficient that \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{D}\); that would make the generative model a lookup table. We want the model to generalize: to produce points that \(p\) assigns high probability to, including points never seen in \(\mathcal{D}\).

The correct criterion is distributional: the empirical distribution of infinitely many samples from our model should converge to \(p(\mathbf{x})\). In other words, if \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(1)}, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(2)}, \ldots\) are generated by our model, then for any measurable \(A\),

\[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^m \mathbf{1}[\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(j)} \in A] \xrightarrow{m \to \infty} p(A)\]

This rules out trivial solutions (like memorizing \(\mathcal{D}\)) and sets the correct target for learning.

docs.title: 66.5 The Universal Starting Point: Uniform Sampling
docs.text:
Every generative model, regardless of its architecture or family, rests on a single foundational assumption:

Assumption 66.1. We know how to sample from the uniform distribution \(\text{Uniform}[0,1]\).

This assumption is taken as given. In practice, it is satisfied by pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) — deterministic algorithms that produce sequences of numbers statistically indistinguishable from \(\text{Uniform}[0,1]\) samples. The famous Mersenne Twister, linear congruential generators, and modern cryptographic PRNGs all serve this purpose. There is a deep irony here: we produce "randomness" using purely deterministic computation.

Everything else — sampling from a Gaussian, a Gamma, a Dirichlet, or a learned neural network distribution — reduces to: 1. Draw \(Z \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\) (or a vector \(\mathbf{Z} \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]^k\)), and 2. Apply a deterministic transformation \(G\) to \(\mathbf{Z}\) so that \(G(\mathbf{Z})\) has the target distribution.

The question is: what should \(G\) be?

docs.title: 66.6 The Fundamental Theorem of Generative Modeling
docs.text: We now state and prove the core result that underlies all generative modeling.

docs.title: 66.6.1 The Push-Forward Principle
docs.text:
Theorem 66.1 (Push-Forward Principle). Let \(\mathbf{Z} \sim p_Z(\mathbf{z})\) be a random variable on \(\mathcal{Z}\), and let \(G_\theta : \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}\) be a (measurable) deterministic function parameterized by \(\theta\). Define \(\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = G_\theta(\mathbf{Z})\). Then \(\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\) is a random variable on \(\mathcal{X}\), and its distribution \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\) — the push-forward of \(p_Z\) through \(G_\theta\) — is generally different from \(p_Z\).

Proof sketch. \(\tilde{X}\) is a random variable because it is the composition of two measurable functions: \(\omega \mapsto Z(\omega)\) and \(G_\theta\). The composite \(\omega \mapsto G_\theta(Z(\omega))\) is measurable, hence a random variable. For any measurable \(A \subseteq \mathcal{X}\),

\[p_\theta(A) = \mathbb{P}(\tilde{X} \in A) = \mathbb{P}(Z \in G_\theta^{-1}(A)) = p_Z(G_\theta^{-1}(A))\]

which in general differs from \(p_Z(A)\) unless \(G_\theta\) is the identity. \(\blacksquare\)

Consequence. If we can find \(\theta^*\) such that the push-forward distribution \(p_{\theta^*}\) matches the target \(p(\mathbf{x})\), then the following procedure samples from \(p(\mathbf{x})\):

Generative Sampling Procedure: 1. Draw \(\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z(\mathbf{z})\) (our known distribution, e.g. \(\text{Uniform}[0,1]^k\) or \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\)). 2. Compute \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = G_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{z})\). 3. Output \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\).

This is the entire intellectual foundation of generative modeling.

docs.title: 66.6.2 Finding \(\theta^*\): Divergence Minimization
docs.text:
How do we find \(\theta^*\)? We minimize a divergence (or dissimilarity measure) between \(p_\theta\) and \(p\):

\[\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \, D(p \,\|\, p_\theta)\]

where \(D(\cdot \,\|\, \cdot)\) is some measure of discrepancy between distributions. Different choices of \(D\) give different generative model families:

Choice of \(D\) Model Family KL divergence \(D_{\text{KL}}(p \| p_\theta)\) Maximum Likelihood / VAE (ELBO) Jensen-Shannon divergence GAN (original formulation) Wasserstein distance Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) \(f\)-divergences \(f\)-GAN family Score matching Score-based / Diffusion models

The design choices reduce to three fundamental questions:

  1. What is \(G_\theta\)? (Architecture of the generator)
  2. What is \(D\)? (Which divergence to minimize)
  3. How do we solve \(\min_\theta D(p \| p_\theta)\)? (Optimization, given that we cannot evaluate \(p\))

The entire literature on generative models is, in a precise sense, a catalogue of different answers to these three questions.

docs.title: 66.7 The Inverse CDF Method: A First Concrete Example
docs.text: To make the Push-Forward Principle concrete before we introduce neural networks, we examine the classical Inverse CDF (Probability Integral Transform) method. It is the simplest instance of the framework and illuminates why the general approach works.

docs.title: 66.7.1 The Probability Integral Transform
docs.text:
Theorem 66.2 (Probability Integral Transform). Let \(X\) be a continuous random variable with CDF \(F_X(x) = \mathbb{P}(X \leq x)\). Define \(U = F_X(X)\). Then \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\).

Proof. For \(u \in [0,1]\):

\[\mathbb{P}(U \leq u) = \mathbb{P}(F_X(X) \leq u) = \mathbb{P}(X \leq F_X^{-1}(u)) = F_X(F_X^{-1}(u)) = u\]

where the third equality uses the fact that \(F_X\) is non-decreasing and the last equality uses the definition of the inverse. Since \(\mathbb{P}(U \leq u) = u\) for all \(u \in [0,1]\), we have \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\). \(\blacksquare\)

docs.title: 66.7.2 The Inverse Transform Method (Sampling)
docs.text:
Corollary 66.1 (Inverse Transform Sampling). Let \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\) and define \(X = F^{-1}(U)\), where \(F\) is the CDF of a target distribution. Then \(X\) has CDF \(F\).

Proof.

\[\mathbb{P}(X \leq x) = \mathbb{P}(F^{-1}(U) \leq x) = \mathbb{P}(U \leq F(x)) = F(x)\]

where the last equality uses \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\). \(\blacksquare\)

This is exactly the Push-Forward Principle with \(\mathcal{Z} = [0,1]\), \(p_Z = \text{Uniform}[0,1]\), and \(G_\theta = F^{-1}\) (the quantile function of the target).

Worked Example 66.1: Sampling from an Exponential Distribution.

Let the target be \(X \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda)\), with PDF \(p(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) for \(x \geq 0\).

Step 1: Compute the CDF. $\(F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}\)$

Step 2: Invert. $\(u = 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \implies e^{-\lambda x} = 1 - u \implies x = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-u)\)$

So \(G = F^{-1}(u) = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-u)\).

Step 3: Sample. Draw \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\) and compute \(X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-U)\).

Since \(1-U \stackrel{d}{=} U\) for \(U \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\), in practice we use \(X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(U)\) for simplicity.

Verification via Change of Variables. Let \(X = G(U) = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(U)\). The PDF of \(X\) is: $\(p_X(x) = p_U(G^{-1}(x)) \cdot \left|\frac{d}{dx}G^{-1}(x)\right|\)$ where \(G^{-1}(x) = e^{-\lambda x}\) and \(\frac{d}{dx}e^{-\lambda x} = -\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\).

\[p_X(x) = 1 \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \checkmark\]

docs.title: 66.7.3 Limitations and the Motivation for Learned \(G_\theta\)
docs.text:
The inverse CDF method requires that we know \(F\) (and therefore \(p\)) in closed form, and that \(F^{-1}\) is computable. Neither condition holds for high-dimensional data distributions like the distribution of natural images, text, or audio.

For a \(512 \times 512\) color image, \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{786432}\). We have no formula for \(p(\mathbf{x})\). What we have is \(\mathcal{D}\). This is the gap that modern generative models bridge: they learn the function \(G_\theta\) from data, using a parametric family (neural networks) flexible enough to approximate any measurable \(G\).

docs.title: 66.8 Generalization of the Inverse CDF: Learned Generators
docs.text:
The Inverse CDF method identified the right structure: - Start from a simple distribution \(p_Z\) (Uniform). - Apply a deterministic function \(G\) (the quantile function). - The result has the target distribution.

The leap to modern generative models is to replace the analytically computed \(G = F^{-1}\) with a learned function \(G_\theta\), parameterized by a deep neural network, trained to minimize \(D(p \| p_\theta)\).

docs.title: 66.8.1 Why Any Measurable \(G_\theta\) Works in Principle
docs.text:
Theorem 66.3 (Universality). For any two distributions \(p_Z\) on \(\mathcal{Z}\) and \(p\) on \(\mathcal{X}\), there exists a measurable function \(G^* : \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}\) such that if \(\mathbf{Z} \sim p_Z\), then \(G^*(\mathbf{Z}) \sim p\).

This is guaranteed by the universal property of measurable functions and the Borel isomorphism theorem for Polish spaces. It tells us that the space of measurable functions is rich enough — in principle, \(G^*\) exists. The practical question is how to find it from data.

Remark. \(G_\theta\) is typically chosen to be a neural network, because neural networks are universal approximators: they can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision (Hornik, 1991). Combined with Theorem 66.3, this means neural networks are expressive enough to represent any generator \(G^*\).

docs.title: 66.8.2 The Change-of-Variables Formula for Neural Generators
docs.text:
When \(G_\theta : \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^d\) is differentiable and bijective (with \(k = d\)), the density of \(\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = G_\theta(\mathbf{Z})\) is given exactly by the change-of-variables formula:

\[p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = p_Z(G_\theta^{-1}(\mathbf{x})) \cdot \left|\det J_{G_\theta^{-1}}(\mathbf{x})\right|\]

where \(J_{G_\theta^{-1}}\) is the Jacobian matrix of \(G_\theta^{-1}\). This formula is the engine of the Normalizing Flows family of generative models (which we will not cover in this introductory chapter, but which appear in the full generative modeling course).

When \(G_\theta\) is not invertible or when \(k < d\) (the generator maps from a lower-dimensional latent space to a higher-dimensional data space), the change-of-variables formula does not apply directly, and we need alternative ways to define or approximate the divergence \(D(p \| p_\theta)\). This is precisely the setting where GANs and VAEs come in.

docs.title: 66.9 Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
docs.text: We now introduce the first of the three major generative model families: Generative Adversarial Networks, proposed by Goodfellow et al. (2014).

docs.title: 66.9.1 Setup and Motivation
docs.text:
Let \(\mathbf{Z} \sim p_Z(\mathbf{z})\), typically \(\mathbf{Z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) (a \(k\)-dimensional standard normal, called the latent space). Let \(G_\theta : \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^d\) be the generator, a neural network. The generator defines an implicit distribution \(p_\theta\) on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) via the push-forward.

We want to find \(\theta\) such that \(p_\theta \approx p\). The challenge: we cannot compute \(p_\theta\) in closed form when \(G_\theta\) is a deep network with \(k < d\), so we cannot directly minimize \(D_{\text{KL}}(p \| p_\theta)\) either.

The GAN Idea. Instead of estimating the density \(p_\theta\) directly, train a second neural network — the discriminator \(D_\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \to [0,1]\) — to distinguish samples from \(p_\theta\) (fake) from samples from \(p\) (real). Use the discriminator's signal to train the generator \(G_\theta\).

docs.title: 66.9.2 The GAN Objective: Deriving the Value Function
docs.text:
We will derive the GAN objective from first principles, starting from the Jensen-Shannon divergence.

Definition 66.2 (Jensen-Shannon Divergence). For distributions \(p\) and \(q\):

\[D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(p \,\Big\|\, \frac{p+q}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(q \,\Big\|\, \frac{p+q}{2}\right)\]

\(D_{\text{JS}}\) is symmetric, bounded in \([0, \ln 2]\), and equals \(0\) if and only if \(p = q\).

Claim. The GAN minimax game is equivalent to minimizing \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta)\).

To see this, consider the following minimax optimization:

\[\min_\theta \max_\phi \, V(G_\theta, D_\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[\ln D_\phi(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[\ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))]\]

Step 1: Optimal discriminator for fixed \(G_\theta\).

Fix \(G_\theta\) and maximize \(V\) over \(D_\phi\). The discriminator sees each point \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d\) and must output a value. The objective decomposes point-wise:

\[\max_{D_\phi} \int_{\mathcal{X}} \left[ p(\mathbf{x}) \ln D_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \ln(1 - D_\phi(\mathbf{x})) \right] d\mathbf{x}\]

For a fixed \(\mathbf{x}\), maximizing \(a \ln y + b \ln(1-y)\) over \(y \in [0,1]\) gives:

\[\frac{d}{dy}[a \ln y + b \ln(1-y)] = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1-y} = 0 \implies y^* = \frac{a}{a+b}\]

Therefore, the optimal discriminator is:

\[\boxed{D_{\phi^*}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}}\]

Interpretation. At the optimal discriminator, \(D_{\phi^*}(\mathbf{x})\) is exactly the posterior probability that \(\mathbf{x}\) came from \(p\) (real) rather than \(p_\theta\) (fake). If \(p = p_\theta\), then \(D_{\phi^*}(\mathbf{x}) = 1/2\) everywhere — the discriminator cannot tell real from fake.

Step 2: Substituting \(D_{\phi^*}\) into \(V\).

\[V(G_\theta, D_{\phi^*}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}\!\left[\ln \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_\theta}\!\left[\ln \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}\right]\]

Adding and subtracting \(\ln 2\) inside each expectation:

\[= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}\!\left[\ln \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{\frac{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{2}} - \ln 2\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_\theta}\!\left[\ln \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{\frac{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{2}} - \ln 2\right]\] \[= D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(p \,\Big\|\, \frac{p + p_\theta}{2}\right) + D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(p_\theta \,\Big\|\, \frac{p + p_\theta}{2}\right) - 2\ln 2\] \[= 2 \cdot D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta) - 2\ln 2\]

Therefore:

\[\min_\theta \max_\phi V(G_\theta, D_\phi) = \min_\theta \left[2 \cdot D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta) - 2\ln 2\right]\]

Minimizing over \(\theta\) minimizes \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta)\), which achieves its minimum value of \(0\) if and only if \(p_\theta = p\). At that point, \(V^* = -2\ln 2\).

Summary: The GAN framework in three lines.

\[\max_\phi \, V = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[\ln D_\phi(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[\ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))] \quad \text{(Discriminator step)}\] \[\min_\theta \, V = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[\ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))] \quad \text{(Generator step)}\] \[\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = G_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{z}), \quad \mathbf{z} \sim p_Z \quad \text{(Sampling)}\]

docs.title: 66.9.3 Worked Example: GAN on a 1D Gaussian
docs.text:
To build intuition without high-dimensional complexity, consider \(p = \mathcal{N}(5, 1)\) (target) and \(p_Z = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) (latent). Let \(G_\theta(z) = \theta_1 z + \theta_2\) (affine generator). Then:

\[p_\theta = \mathcal{N}(\theta_2, \theta_1^2)\]

The true distribution is \(\mathcal{N}(5, 1)\), so we need \(\theta_1^* = 1\) (scale) and \(\theta_2^* = 5\) (shift).

The GAN would train a discriminator \(D_\phi : \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]\) to distinguish samples from \(\mathcal{N}(5,1)\) from samples of \(G_\theta(z) = \theta_1 z + \theta_2\) for \(z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\). The generator \(G_\theta\) is trained to fool \(D_\phi\) — i.e., to make \(D_\phi(G_\theta(z)) \approx 1\) for all \(z\).

At convergence, \(D_\phi(x) = 1/2\) for all \(x\), and \(\tilde{x} = G_{\theta^*}(z) = z + 5 \sim \mathcal{N}(5, 1) = p\). The generator has learned to sample from the target by learning the correct affine map from the known latent to the unknown target.

docs.title: 66.9.4 Training Procedure and Practical Considerations
docs.text:
In practice, we do not have access to \(p\) or \(p_\theta\) in closed form — only to finite samples. The GAN training loop approximates the expectations with mini-batch averages:

Algorithm 66.1: GAN Training (Mini-batch SGD)

For each training iteration: 1. Sample a mini-batch of real data: \(\{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m \sim p\) 2. Sample a mini-batch of latent vectors: \(\{\mathbf{z}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m \sim p_Z\) 3. Compute discriminator loss: $\(\hat{L}_D = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \left[\ln D_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + \ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z}^{(i)})))\right]\)$ 4. Update \(\phi\) by gradient descent: \(\phi \leftarrow \phi - \eta \nabla_\phi \hat{L}_D\) 5. Sample fresh \(\{\mathbf{z}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m \sim p_Z\) 6. Compute generator loss (equivalently, use the non-saturating variant): $\(\hat{L}_G = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \ln D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))\)$ 7. Update \(\theta\) by gradient descent: \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \hat{L}_G\)

Remark on the non-saturating loss. The original generator loss \(\mathbb{E}[\ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))]\) saturates early in training: when \(D_\phi\) confidently rejects \(G_\theta\)'s outputs, \(D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})) \approx 0\), and \(\ln(1-0) = 0\) produces vanishingly small gradients. The fix is to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\ln D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z}))]\) instead (equivalently, minimize the negative), which provides stronger gradients even when the discriminator is winning. This is the most common formulation in practice.

docs.title: 66.10 Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
docs.text: The second major family uses a different answer to our three questions: explicit density estimation via a lower bound, with a learned encoder-decoder architecture.

docs.title: 66.10.1 The Problem: Intractable Marginals
docs.text:
We want to model \(p(\mathbf{x})\) using a latent variable \(\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^k\). The generative story is:

\[\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}) \quad \text{(prior on latent)}$$ $$\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z} \sim p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) \quad \text{(decoder / likelihood)}\]

The marginal likelihood is:

\[p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) \, p(\mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{z}\]

This integral is intractable for deep neural network decoders \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})\), because the latent space is high-dimensional and the integrand is not in closed form.

Direct maximum likelihood would require:

\[\max_\theta \sum_{i=1}^n \ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \max_\theta \sum_{i=1}^n \ln \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{z}) \, p(\mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{z}\]

which is intractable. We need a surrogate.

docs.title: 66.10.2 The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
docs.text:
Introduce an approximate posterior \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})\) — a neural network that maps data \(\mathbf{x}\) to a distribution over latent codes \(\mathbf{z}\). This is the encoder.

For any \(q_\phi\), by Jensen's inequality:

\[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \ln \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{z}\] \[= \ln \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \cdot \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})} \, d\mathbf{z}\] \[= \ln \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\!\left[\frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\right]\] \[\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\!\left[\ln \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\right] \quad \text{(Jensen, since }\ln\text{ is concave)}\]

This lower bound is the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO):

\[\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; \mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))\]

Derivation of the KL form. Expand the expectation:

\[\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}\!\left[\ln \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\right] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})] + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}\!\left[\ln \frac{p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}\right]\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))\]

Interpretation of the two terms:

The reconstruction term \(\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})]\) measures how well the decoder \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})\) can reconstruct \(\mathbf{x}\) from latent codes sampled from the encoder. It rewards the encoder-decoder pair for faithfully representing the data.

The regularization term \(-D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))\) penalizes the encoder's posterior \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})\) for deviating from the prior \(p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\). This keeps the latent space well-organized and ensures that we can sample \(\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z})\) and decode it to a plausible \(\mathbf{x}\).

The exact gap. We can show:

\[\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; \mathbf{x}) + D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \| p_\theta(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}))\]

Since \(D_{\text{KL}} \geq 0\), we confirm \(\mathcal{L} \leq \ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\), with equality when \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) = p_\theta(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})\) (the approximate posterior equals the true posterior).

docs.title: 66.10.3 The Reparameterization Trick
docs.text:
To train both \(\theta\) and \(\phi\) jointly by gradient descent, we need \(\nabla_\phi \mathcal{L}\). The difficulty: the expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}[\cdot]\) depends on \(\phi\) (the distribution we sample from), so we cannot simply push the gradient through the expectation.

Solution: The Reparameterization Trick. Choose \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}), \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2_\phi(\mathbf{x})))\), where \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi\) and \(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi\) are outputs of the encoder neural network. Instead of sampling \(\mathbf{z} \sim q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})\) directly, write:

\[\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\]

Now \(\mathbf{z}\) is a deterministic function of \(\phi\), \(\mathbf{x}\), and \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\) (which does not depend on \(\phi\)). Therefore:

\[\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})] = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})}[f(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon})]\]

and we can compute \(\nabla_\phi\) by differentiating through \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi\) and \(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi\).

docs.title: 66.10.4 Closed-Form KL for Gaussian Prior and Posterior
docs.text:
When both prior \(p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\) and posterior \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2))\) are Gaussian, the KL term has a closed form:

\[D_{\text{KL}}(\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)) \| \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^k \left(\mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - 1 - \ln \sigma_j^2\right)\]

Derivation (for scalar case, \(k=1\)). Let \(q = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) and \(p = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\).

\[D_{\text{KL}}(q \| p) = \int q(z) \ln \frac{q(z)}{p(z)} \, dz = \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[\ln q(z) - \ln p(z)\right]\] \[= \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) + \frac{z^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi)\right]\] \[= -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mathbb{E}_q[z^2]}{2}\]

Since \(\mathbb{E}_q[z^2] = \text{Var}_q(z) + (\mathbb{E}_q[z])^2 = \sigma^2 + \mu^2\):

\[D_{\text{KL}}(q \| p) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\mu^2 + \sigma^2 - 1 - \ln\sigma^2\right) \checkmark\]

The vector case follows by independence across dimensions.

docs.title: 66.10.5 VAE Sampling at Test Time
docs.text:
Once training is complete, to generate a new sample \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\):

  1. Draw \(\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\) (no encoder needed at test time).
  2. Decode: \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{z})\).

This is exactly the Push-Forward framework: a noise vector \(\mathbf{z}\) from a known distribution is passed through a learned deterministic function (the decoder) to produce a sample from (approximately) \(p(\mathbf{x})\).

docs.title: 66.11 Autoregressive Models
docs.text: The third family approaches the problem from a completely different direction: explicit density estimation using the chain rule of probability.

docs.title: 66.11.1 The Chain Rule Decomposition
docs.text:
Any joint distribution over \(d\) variables can be factored as:

\[p(\mathbf{x}) = p(x_1) \cdot p(x_2 \mid x_1) \cdot p(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2) \cdots p(x_d \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}) = \prod_{j=1}^d p(x_j \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{j-1})\]

This is not an approximation — it is an exact identity from the definition of conditional probability. It holds for any ordering of the variables and any distribution \(p\).

An autoregressive model parameterizes each conditional:

\[p_\theta(x_j \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{j-1}) = f_\theta^{(j)}(x_1, \ldots, x_{j-1})\]

where \(f_\theta^{(j)}\) is a neural network that takes the previous \(j-1\) variables as input and outputs a distribution over \(x_j\).

docs.title: 66.11.2 Autoregressive Sampling
docs.text:
Sampling from an autoregressive model is sequential:

  1. Sample \(x_1 \sim p_\theta(x_1)\) (an unconditional distribution).
  2. Sample \(x_2 \sim p_\theta(x_2 \mid x_1)\).
  3. Sample \(x_j \sim p_\theta(x_j \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{j-1})\) for \(j = 3, \ldots, d\).
  4. Return \(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)\).

Each step uses the Push-Forward principle: given \(u \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1]\), apply the inverse CDF of \(p_\theta(x_j \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{j-1})\) to get \(x_j\).

docs.title: 66.11.3 Language Models as Autoregressive Samplers
docs.text:
In the language modeling setting, \(\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)\) is a sequence of tokens (subword units) from a vocabulary \(\mathcal{V}\) with \(|\mathcal{V}| \approx 50{,}000\). The model factorizes:

\[p_\theta(x_1, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^T p_\theta(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})\]

Each conditional \(p_\theta(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})\) is a categorical distribution over \(|\mathcal{V}|\) tokens, output by a Transformer network.

Training objective. Maximize the log-likelihood over the training corpus:

\[\max_\theta \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \ln p_\theta(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_1^{(i)}, \ldots, x_{t-1}^{(i)})\]

This is equivalent to minimizing the cross-entropy between the empirical distribution of the corpus and \(p_\theta\), which in turn minimizes \(D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_\theta)\).

Conditional generation. A prompted LLM computes \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y})\) where \(\mathbf{y}\) is the prompt. The factorization continues from position \(T_y + 1\):

\[p_\theta(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \prod_{t=T_y+1}^{T} p_\theta(x_t \mid y_1, \ldots, y_{T_y}, x_{T_y+1}, \ldots, x_{t-1})\]

Each call to the model is still a categorical distribution over \(|\mathcal{V}|\) tokens, and sampling is just drawing from that categorical.

docs.title: 66.11.4 Example: Next-Token Prediction
docs.text:
Suppose \(\mathcal{V} = \{A, B, C\}\) and we observe the corpus \(\mathcal{D} = \{\text{"ABACABA"}\}\) (treated as 7 tokens: \(A, B, A, C, A, B, A\)).

A perfect autoregressive model would learn:

Context Next token distribution (none) \(p(x_1 = A) = 1\) \(A\) \(p(B \mid A) = 2/3, \; p(C \mid A) = 1/3\) \(AB\) \(p(A \mid AB) = 1\) \(ABA\) \(p(C \mid ABA) = 1\) \(ABAC\) \(p(A \mid ABAC) = 1\) \(ABACA\) \(p(B \mid ABACA) = 1\) \(ABACAB\) \(p(A \mid ABACAB) = 1\)

Sampling from this model: Draw \(x_1 = A\). Draw \(x_2 \sim p(\cdot \mid A) = \text{Categorical}(B: 2/3, C: 1/3)\). If \(x_2 = B\): draw \(x_3 = A\). Etc. This produces sequences that follow the statistical patterns of the training corpus, but generalizes beyond it (e.g., could generate "ABACA" which was not explicitly a training sequence).

docs.title: 66.12 Evaluating Generative Models
docs.text: A critical subtlety: unlike discriminative models, we cannot evaluate a generative model by prediction accuracy on a held-out test set, because there are no labels. How do we measure whether \(p_\theta \approx p\)?

docs.title: 66.12.1 Test Set Log-Likelihood
docs.text:
For explicit models (autoregressive, VAE), we can evaluate the held-out log-likelihood:

\[\text{NLL} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}|} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}} \ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\]

This is the gold standard when tractable. A lower NLL indicates \(p_\theta\) assigns higher probability to real data — it has learned the distribution better. Note that the NLL minimization target is exactly \(D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_\theta)\) up to a constant (the entropy of \(p_{\text{data}}\)):

\[D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_\theta) = H(p_{\text{data}}) + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[-\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x})] = \text{const} + \text{NLL}\]

For implicit models like GANs, there is no tractable \(\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\), so NLL is unavailable.

docs.title: 66.12.2 Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
docs.text:
FID is the most common quantitative metric for image generative models. The idea: embed both real and generated images into a feature space using a pretrained Inception network, and measure the distance between the two distributions of embeddings.

Formally, let \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_r, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_r\) be the mean and covariance of real image features and \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_g, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_g\) for generated features. The FID is the Fréchet (Wasserstein-2) distance between two Gaussians fit to these embeddings:

\[\text{FID} = \|\boldsymbol{\mu}_r - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g\|^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_r + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_g - 2(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_r \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_g)^{1/2}\right)\]

FID equals \(0\) when \(p_\theta = p\) (in the feature space). Lower is better. It can be computed for any model from which samples can be drawn, including GANs.

Caveat. FID measures distributional similarity in a particular feature space (Inception features). A model could have low FID while still producing artifacts invisible to that feature extractor. It is a practical proxy, not a perfect measure of \(D(p \| p_\theta)\).

docs.title: 66.12.3 Overfitting in Generative Models
docs.text:
Recall the overfitting problem in discriminative models: a model that memorizes the training set achieves zero training loss but fails on new data. The analog in generative models is a model that simply stores \(\mathcal{D}\) and returns a random \(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\) when asked to sample.

Such a model would have very low (or zero) NLL on training data, but: - Would never generalize to points not in \(\mathcal{D}\). - Would assign zero probability to any \(\mathbf{x} \notin \mathcal{D}\). - In a continuous space, every sample from a good \(p_\theta\) should have \(\mathbb{P}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = 0\) for any specific \(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{D}\) (since the probability of a single point under a continuous distribution is zero).

The correct behavior is distributional: the model should put high density in regions where \(p(\mathbf{x})\) is high, not just on the specific samples it has seen. A held-out test set helps detect this, as a memorizing model will assign low likelihood to test points.

docs.title: 66.13 Connecting the Three Families: A Unified View
docs.text:
All three families — GANs, VAEs, and autoregressive models — instantiate the same underlying framework:

\[\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta D(p \| p_\theta), \quad \tilde{\mathbf{x}} = G_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{z}), \quad \mathbf{z} \sim p_Z\]

They differ in the choices of \(G_\theta\), \(D\), and how the optimization is carried out:

GAN VAE Autoregressive \(p_Z\) \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) \(\text{Uniform}[0,1]^d\) (via inverse CDF at each step) \(G_\theta\) Deep network (implicit) Decoder network Sequential conditionals \(D\) Jensen-Shannon (approximated adversarially) KL (bounded via ELBO) KL (exact, via log-likelihood) Density \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\) tractable? No No (ELBO is a bound) Yes Sampling One forward pass One forward pass \(d\) sequential forward passes Training stability Challenging (minimax) Stable (ELBO gradient) Stable (cross-entropy)

This table is not just a summary — it reflects deep mathematical trade-offs. Explicit density estimation (autoregressive) gives tractable likelihoods but requires sequential sampling, which is slow for long sequences. Implicit estimation (GAN) enables fast one-shot sampling but provides no likelihood and is notoriously difficult to train due to the minimax objective. VAEs occupy a middle ground: they provide a tractable lower bound on the likelihood and fast sampling, but the bound is loose when the approximate posterior \(q_\phi\) is far from the true posterior.

docs.title: 66.14 Summary
docs.text:
This chapter established the mathematical foundations of generative modeling. The key ideas are:

The goal of generative modeling is strictly stronger than density estimation: we must not only approximate \(p(\mathbf{x})\), but also be able to sample from it efficiently.

The fundamental principle is the Push-Forward: learn a function \(G_{\theta^*} : \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}\) such that \(G_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{Z}) \sim p(\mathbf{x})\) for \(\mathbf{Z} \sim p_Z\), where \(p_Z\) is a known, easy-to-sample distribution. Finding \(\theta^*\) is cast as divergence minimization: \(\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta D(p \| p_\theta)\).

The Inverse CDF method is the simplest concrete instance: \(G = F^{-1}\) (the quantile function). It works for known 1D distributions but fails for unknown high-dimensional distributions, motivating learned generators.

GANs use an adversarial discriminator to approximate the Jensen-Shannon divergence without needing to compute \(p_\theta\) in closed form. The minimax game yields \(D_{\phi^*}(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x})/(p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x}))\), and optimizing the generator is equivalent to minimizing \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta)\).

VAEs use a latent variable model with an approximate posterior (encoder) and a decoder, trained by maximizing the ELBO — a tractable lower bound on \(\ln p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\) derived via Jensen's inequality. The reparameterization trick enables gradient flow through the sampling operation.

Autoregressive models exploit the exact chain rule factorization \(p(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_j p(x_j \mid x_{< j})\), modeling each conditional with a neural network and sampling sequentially. Language models are the preeminent example.

All three families answer the same three questions differently: what is \(G_\theta\), what is \(D\), and how is the optimization solved. The field of deep generative modeling is, in essence, the study of increasingly sophisticated answers to these three questions.

End of Chapter 66. The following chapter examines training instabilities in GANs and the Wasserstein GAN formulation, which replaces the Jensen-Shannon divergence with the Wasserstein-1 distance to address the vanishing gradient problem.

docs.title: 67.1 Recapitulation and Roadmap
docs.text:
In the previous chapter we established the master framework of generative modeling. Every generative model is, at bottom, a solution to the following problem:

\[\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \, D(p \,\|\, p_\theta), \qquad \tilde{\mathbf{x}} = G_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{z}), \quad \mathbf{z} \sim p_Z \tag{67.1}\]

where \(p\) is the unknown data distribution, \(p_\theta\) is the push-forward of a known noise distribution \(p_Z\) through a parametric function \(G_\theta\), and \(D\) is some divergence measure.

Three questions govern every specific model: 1. What is \(p_Z\)? 2. What is \(G_\theta\)? 3. What is \(D\), and how is \(\min_\theta D(p \| p_\theta)\) solved given that neither \(p\) nor \(p_\theta\) is available in closed form?

For Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), the answers are: 1. \(p_Z = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) — a \(k\)-dimensional standard normal (rather than Uniform, for reasons discussed in §67.2). 2. \(G_\theta\) is a deep neural network, typically with an expanding architecture (mapping \(\mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^d\) with \(k < d\)). 3. \(D\) belongs to the family of \(f\)-divergences, specifically the Jensen-Shannon divergence. Because this divergence is intractable, we construct a variational lower bound on it; tightening that bound introduces a second neural network \(D_\phi\), the discriminator (or critic). The result is a saddle-point optimization problem over \((\theta, \phi)\).

This chapter builds the GAN formulation from the ground up: from the choice of \(p_Z\), through the \(f\)-divergence family and its variational representation, to the minimax objective, the training algorithm, and a careful analysis of what the optimal solution means.

docs.title: 67.2 Why \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\) Instead of Uniform?
docs.text:
In Chapter 66 we took \(p_Z = \text{Uniform}[0,1]\) as the canonical starting point, motivated by the Probability Integral Transform. In practice, however, generative models for continuous data use \(p_Z = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\).

The reason is support. A \(k\)-dimensional uniform distribution has support \([0,1]^k\), a bounded hypercube. Natural data distributions — images, audio, text embeddings — have unbounded support in \(\mathbb{R}^d\): any value, however extreme, occurs with some nonzero probability. If \(p_Z\) has bounded support and \(G_\theta\) is a continuous function, then by the continuous image theorem \(G_\theta([0,1]^k)\) is also bounded, and \(p_\theta\) cannot assign nonzero density to unbounded regions.

\(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) has full support on \(\mathbb{R}^k\): for every open set \(U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k\), \(p_Z(U) > 0\). Therefore, \(p_\theta\) can in principle place mass anywhere in \(\mathbb{R}^d\), matching the full support of \(p\).

Moreover, we can sample from \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) for the same reason we can sample from Uniform\([0,1]\): there exists a known deterministic transformation from \(\text{Uniform}[0,1]^k\) to \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\), called the Box-Muller transform (one concrete instance of the Push-Forward Principle itself).

Box-Muller Transform (Example 67.1). Given \(U_1, U_2 \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Uniform}(0,1]\), define:

\[Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2), \qquad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)\]

Then \(Z_1, Z_2 \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,1)\). The full derivation uses the change-of-variables formula for the joint density of \((U_1, U_2)\) under the polar coordinate transformation, which the reader can verify as an exercise. The key takeaway is that sampling from \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_k)\) is itself an instance of the master framework — we will simply accept it as a primitive going forward.

docs.title: 67.3 The \(f\)-Divergence Family
docs.text: The choice of divergence \(D\) in (67.1) is not arbitrary — it fundamentally shapes the geometry of the optimization landscape and the behavior of the learned model. GANs use a large and principled family called \(f\)-divergences, which contains the KL divergence, reverse KL, Jensen-Shannon, \(\chi^2\), and many others as special cases.

docs.title: 67.3.1 Definition
docs.text:
Definition 67.1 (\(f\)-Divergence). Let \(f : (0, \infty) \to \mathbb{R}\) be a convex, lower-semicontinuous function with \(f(1) = 0\). The \(f\)-divergence between distributions \(p\) and \(q\) (with \(p \ll q\), i.e., \(p\) is absolutely continuous with respect to \(q\)) is:

\[D_f(p \,\|\, q) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} q(\mathbf{x}) \, f\!\left(\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})}\right) d\mathbf{x} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim q}\!\left[f\!\left(\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})}\right)\right] \tag{67.2}\]

The function \(f\) is called the generator function of the divergence. (Beware the overloaded terminology: in the GAN context, "generator" refers to the network \(G_\theta\); here it is a scalar function defining the divergence. These are distinct uses of the same word.)

Properties. For any valid \(f\): - \(D_f(p \| q) \geq 0\) for all \(p, q\). - \(D_f(p \| q) = 0\) if and only if \(p = q\) (almost everywhere). - \(D_f\) is generally not symmetric: \(D_f(p \| q) \neq D_f(q \| p)\) in general.

The non-negativity follows from Jensen's inequality: since \(f\) is convex,

\[\mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[f\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\right] \geq f\!\left(\mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\frac{p}{q}\right]\right) = f\!\left(\int p(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x}\right) = f(1) = 0\]

where we used \(\int p(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = 1\).

docs.title: 67.3.2 Special Cases
docs.text:
The following table shows how the standard divergences arise as special cases.

\(f(t)\) \(D_f(p \| q)\) Name \(t \ln t\) \(D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = \int p \ln \frac{p}{q}\) KL divergence (forward) \(-\ln t\) \(D_{\text{KL}}(q \| p) = \int q \ln \frac{q}{p}\) Reverse KL divergence \((t-1)^2\) \(\chi^2(p \| q) = \int \frac{(p-q)^2}{q}\) Chi-squared divergence \(\frac{1}{2}\left(t \ln t - (t+1)\ln\frac{t+1}{2}\right)\) \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q)\) Jensen-Shannon divergence \(\|t - 1\|\) $TV(p, q) = \frac{1}{2}\int p - q

Worked Example 67.1: Verifying KL as an \(f\)-divergence.

Take \(f(t) = t \ln t\). Then:

\[D_f(p \| q) = \int q(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})} \ln \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})} \, d\mathbf{x} = \int p(\mathbf{x}) \ln \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})} \, d\mathbf{x} = D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) \checkmark\]

Note \(f(1) = 1 \cdot \ln 1 = 0\) ✓ and \(f''(t) = 1/t > 0\) for \(t > 0\), so \(f\) is convex ✓.

docs.title: 67.3.3 Why Not Just Use KL?
docs.text:
This is a natural question. We used \(D_{\text{KL}}\) for all our discriminative models (maximum likelihood minimizes \(D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_\theta)\)), so why not here?

The issue is the support mismatch problem. Recall that \(D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = \int p \ln \frac{p}{q}\). If there exists a region \(A\) where \(p(A) > 0\) but \(q(A) = 0\), then \(\frac{p}{q} \to \infty\) on \(A\) and \(D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = +\infty\). In the generative setting, early in training \(p_\theta\) is randomly initialized and its support may not overlap with \(p\) at all. This makes the KL gradient uninformative — it is \(+\infty\) everywhere that \(p_\theta\) has no support.

Reverse KL \(D_{\text{KL}}(q \| p)\) has the opposite pathology: it is \(+\infty\) when \(q\) assigns mass where \(p\) does not, forcing the model to be zero-avoiding (it must cover everything \(p\) covers) but potentially ignoring modes of \(p\) it hasn't yet found. The two behaviors are called mass-covering (forward KL) and mode-seeking (reverse KL).

Jensen-Shannon divergence \(D_{\text{JS}}\) is a symmetric interpolation between the two, bounded in \([0, \ln 2]\), and well-defined even when \(p\) and \(p_\theta\) have disjoint support (it evaluates to \(\ln 2\) in that case, rather than \(\infty\)). This makes it more numerically stable for early-stage training and provides gradients even when supports do not overlap. These properties motivated Goodfellow et al. (2014) to build the original GAN around \(D_{\text{JS}}\).

docs.title: 67.4 The Variational Representation of \(f\)-Divergences
docs.text:
The central mathematical obstacle is that we cannot compute \(D_f(p \| p_\theta)\) — we know neither \(p\) nor \(p_\theta\) in closed form. We only have samples from each.

The key breakthrough is that every \(f\)-divergence admits a variational lower bound expressible purely in terms of expectations over samples. This is the Fenchel conjugate (or Legendre transform) representation.

docs.title: 67.4.1 The Fenchel Conjugate
docs.text:
Definition 67.2 (Fenchel Conjugate). For a function \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\), its Fenchel conjugate (convex conjugate) is:

\[f^*(s) = \sup_{t \in \text{dom}(f)} \left\{st - f(t)\right\} \tag{67.3}\]

For convex \(f\), the Fenchel-Moreau theorem guarantees that \(f^{**} = f\), i.e., \(f\) is recoverable from \(f^*\):

\[f(t) = \sup_{s \in \text{dom}(f^*)} \left\{st - f^*(s)\right\} \tag{67.4}\]

Example 67.2. For \(f(t) = t \ln t\) (KL generator):

\[f^*(s) = \sup_t \{st - t\ln t\}\]

Setting \(\frac{d}{dt}[st - t\ln t] = s - \ln t - 1 = 0\) gives \(t^* = e^{s-1}\), so:

\[f^*(s) = s \cdot e^{s-1} - e^{s-1}\ln(e^{s-1}) = s e^{s-1} - (s-1)e^{s-1} = e^{s-1}\]

Therefore \(f^*(s) = e^{s-1}\), with domain \(\mathbb{R}\).

docs.title: 67.4.2 The Variational Lower Bound
docs.text:
Theorem 67.1 (Variational Representation of \(f\)-Divergences, Nguyen et al. 2010). For any \(f\)-divergence:

\[D_f(p \,\|\, q) = \sup_{T : \mathcal{X} \to \text{dom}(f^*)} \left\{ \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[T(\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim q}[f^*(T(\mathbf{x}))] \right\} \tag{67.5}\]

where the supremum is over all measurable functions \(T : \mathcal{X} \to \text{dom}(f^*)\).

Proof. Starting from (67.2) and substituting (67.4):

\[D_f(p \| q) = \int q(\mathbf{x}) f\!\left(\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})}\right) d\mathbf{x} = \int q(\mathbf{x}) \sup_s \left\{s \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})} - f^*(s)\right\} d\mathbf{x}\]

Since the supremum over \(s\) can be taken pointwise and \(q > 0\):

\[\geq \sup_T \int q(\mathbf{x}) \left\{T(\mathbf{x}) \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})} - f^*(T(\mathbf{x}))\right\} d\mathbf{x}\] \[= \sup_T \left\{\int p(\mathbf{x}) T(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} - \int q(\mathbf{x}) f^*(T(\mathbf{x})) d\mathbf{x}\right\}\] \[= \sup_T \left\{\mathbb{E}_p[T(\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}_q[f^*(T(\mathbf{x}))]\right\}\]

The inequality becomes an equality when \(T^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\sup_s\{s \cdot p(\mathbf{x})/q(\mathbf{x}) - f^*(s)\}\), which by (67.3)–(67.4) is \(T^*(\mathbf{x}) = (f^*)^{-1}(p(\mathbf{x})/q(\mathbf{x}))\) (or equivalently, where the pointwise supremum is attained). \(\blacksquare\)

What does this tell us? The \(f\)-divergence equals the supremum of a quantity that involves only: - An expectation over \(p\) (estimable from real data \(\mathcal{D}\)), and - An expectation over \(q = p_\theta\) (estimable by generating samples through \(G_\theta\)).

The function \(T\) is the variational parameter. Approximating \(T\) with a neural network \(T_\phi\) — restricting the supremum to the class of functions representable by that network — gives us a computable lower bound:

\[D_f(p \,\|\, p_\theta) \geq \sup_\phi \left\{ \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[T_\phi(\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[f^*(T_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))] \right\} \tag{67.6}\]

This is precisely the bound whose tightness requires solving the inner maximization over \(\phi\). Plugging this bound back into our goal of minimizing \(D_f(p \| p_\theta)\) over \(\theta\) gives the GAN saddle-point problem:

\[\min_\theta \max_\phi \left\{ \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[T_\phi(\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[f^*(T_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))] \right\} \tag{67.7}\]

This is the general \(f\)-GAN objective (Nowozin et al., 2016). The specific functional form of the objective depends on the choice of \(f\). Let us now work out the case \(f = D_{\text{JS}}\) in detail.

docs.title: 67.5.1 The Jensen-Shannon Divergence
docs.text:
Recall from Chapter 66:

\[D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(p \,\Big\|\, \frac{p+q}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(q \,\Big\|\, \frac{p+q}{2}\right) \tag{67.8}\]

We can write this using the mixture distribution \(m = \frac{p+q}{2}\):

\[D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = \frac{1}{2}\int p \ln\frac{p}{m} + \frac{1}{2}\int q \ln\frac{q}{m} = \ln 2 - H\!\left(\frac{p+q}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}H(p) + \frac{1}{2}H(q)\]

where \(H\) denotes differential entropy. Key properties: - \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) \in [0, \ln 2]\), bounded regardless of support overlap. - \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = 0 \Leftrightarrow p = q\). - Symmetric: \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = D_{\text{JS}}(q \| p)\). - \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| q) = \ln 2\) when \(p\) and \(q\) have completely disjoint support.

docs.title: 67.5.2 The Minimax Value Function
docs.text:
In the original GAN paper, Goodfellow et al. (2014) proposed the following value function directly:

\[V(G_\theta, D_\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}[\ln D_\phi(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[\ln(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(\mathbf{z})))] \tag{67.9}\]

where \(D_\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \to (0,1)\) (with a sigmoid output layer). The GAN training objective is:

\[\min_\theta \max_\phi \, V(G_\theta, D_\phi) \tag{67.10}\]

We will now rigorously derive that this is equivalent to minimizing \(D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta)\).

docs.title: 67.5.3 Step 1 — Optimal Discriminator for Fixed \(G_\theta\)
docs.text:
Proposition 67.1. For any fixed generator \(G_\theta\) (and thus fixed \(p_\theta\)), the discriminator that maximizes \(V(G_\theta, D_\phi)\) over all measurable functions \(D : \mathbb{R}^d \to (0,1)\) is:

\[D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})} \tag{67.11}\]

Proof. Since the expectations in (67.9) are over the data space, we can write:

\[V = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \left[ p(\mathbf{x}) \ln D(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \ln(1 - D(\mathbf{x})) \right] d\mathbf{x}\]

(using \(\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_Z}[f(G_\theta(\mathbf{z}))] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_\theta}[f(\mathbf{x})]\) by the push-forward). The integrand decomposes pointwise: for each \(\mathbf{x}\), we maximize \(h(y) = a \ln y + b \ln(1-y)\) over \(y \in (0,1)\) with \(a = p(\mathbf{x}) > 0\) and \(b = p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) > 0\).

\[h'(y) = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1-y} = 0 \implies a(1-y) = by \implies y^* = \frac{a}{a+b} = \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}\]

Checking the second derivative: \(h''(y) = -a/y^2 - b/(1-y)^2 < 0\), confirming this is a maximum. \(\blacksquare\)

Interpretation. \(D^*(\mathbf{x})\) is exactly the Bayes-optimal classifier for the binary problem of distinguishing whether \(\mathbf{x}\) came from \(p\) (real, label \(1\)) or \(p_\theta\) (generated, label \(0\)). When \(p = p_\theta\), \(D^*(\mathbf{x}) = 1/2\) everywhere — the discriminator is no better than a coin flip.

Remark. A discriminator with sigmoid output naturally parameterizes \(D_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \in (0,1)\). In the limit of infinite capacity, \(D_\phi\) can approximate any measurable function, so this optimal solution is achievable.

docs.title: 67.5.4 Step 2 — Substituting \(D^*\) and Connecting to \(D_{\text{JS}}\)
docs.text:
Theorem 67.2. The minimax value of the GAN game equals \(2D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta) - 2\ln 2\):

\[\max_\phi V(G_\theta, D_\phi) = 2 D_{\text{JS}}(p \,\|\, p_\theta) - 2\ln 2 \tag{67.12}\]

Proof. Substitute \(D^*\) from (67.11) into (67.9):

\[V(G_\theta, D^*) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p}\!\left[\ln \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_\theta}\!\left[\ln \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}\right]\]

In each term, add and subtract \(\ln 2\) inside:

\[= \mathbb{E}_{p}\!\left[\ln\frac{2 p(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})} - \ln 2\right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_\theta}\!\left[\ln\frac{2 p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})} - \ln 2\right]\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{p}\!\left[\ln\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{m(\mathbf{x})}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_\theta}\!\left[\ln\frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{m(\mathbf{x})}\right] - 2\ln 2\]

where \(m(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}) + p_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{2}\) is the mixture. Recognizing the KL divergences:

\[= D_{\text{KL}}(p \,\|\, m) + D_{\text{KL}}(p_\theta \,\|\, m) - 2\ln 2 = 2 D_{\text{JS}}(p \,\|\, p_\theta) - 2\ln 2 \qquad \blacksquare\]

Corollary 67.1. The outer minimization

\[\min_\theta \max_\phi V(G_\theta, D_\phi) = \min_\theta \left[2 D_{\text{JS}}(p \,\|\, p_\theta) - 2\ln 2\right]\]

is equivalent to \(\min_\theta D_{\text{JS}}(p \| p_\theta)\), which is minimized (to \(0\)) if and only if \(p_\theta = p\).

At the global optimum: - \(p_{\theta^*} = p\) (the generator perfectly matches the data distribution), - \(D^*(\mathbf{x}) = 1/2\) for all \(\mathbf{x}\) (the discriminator cannot distinguish real from generated), - \(V^* = -2\ln 2 \approx -1.386\).

docs.title: 67.6 The Saddle-Point Structure and Why It Is Hard
67.6.1 What Is a Saddle Point?
docs.text:
Definition 67.3 (Saddle Point). A point \((\theta^*, \phi^*)\) is a saddle point of \(V(\theta, \phi)\) if:

\[V(\theta^*, \phi) \leq V(\theta^*, \phi^*) \leq V(\theta, \phi^*) \quad \forall \, \theta, \phi\]

In other words, \((\theta^*, \phi^*)\) is a minimum of \(V\) with respect to \(\theta\) and simultaneously a maximum with respect to \(\phi\).

Geometrically, imagine the surface of \(V\) over \((\theta, \phi)\)-space. At a saddle point, slicing along the \(\theta\)-axis gives a local minimum, while slicing along the \(\phi\)-axis gives a local maximum — like the surface of a horse's saddle (hence the name). The landscape curves downward in one direction and upward in another.

docs.title: 67.6.2 The Alternating Optimization Strategy
docs.text:
Standard gradient descent seeks a minimum of a function by always moving downhill. For a saddle-point problem, we need to move downhill in \(\theta\) and uphill in \(\phi\) simultaneously. The standard approach is alternating gradient descent-ascent:

For each iteration \(t\): 1. Fix \(\theta\), take a gradient ascent step in \(\phi\): \(\phi \leftarrow \phi + \eta \nabla_\phi V\). 2. Fix \(\phi\), take a gradient descent step in \(\theta\): \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta V\).

This is not guaranteed to converge in general, even for convex-concave problems with non-trivial structure. In the deep neural network setting, where both \(G_\theta\) and \(D_\phi\) are non-convex, convergence guarantees are essentially absent. This fundamental difficulty is the primary reason GAN training is notoriously unstable, prone to oscillation (alternating between regions rather than converging) and mode collapse (the generator producing only a few distinct samples).

docs.title: 67.6.3 Intuitive Interpretation: The Adversarial Game
docs.text:
Although the saddle-point formulation is the correct mathematical view, there is a complementary intuition that has become standard in the field.

Think of \(D_\phi\) as a detective and \(G_\theta\) as a forger. The detective's job is to inspect a piece of data \(\mathbf{x}\) and decide: is this authentic (from \(p\)) or counterfeit (from \(p_\theta\))?
